segments.text: Good morning. Welcome.
Good morning.
Good to have you here.
Thank you. Nice to be here.
We will look forward to a good conversation.
So are we.
You're the first of the three.
Perfect.
Right.
Welcome. Thank you also for responding to some of the
advanced questions.
But I think what we'll do is, as we usually do, hand it over to you for, say, five
minutes or so.
We've already had a bit of a session just sort of talking with some
pre-questions with the
auditors.
And so we'll open it up to you, and then there will be plenty of questions.
Many thanks. Tena koutou katoa. For those who
don't know, I'm Scott St John, the chair of Mercury.
Stu Hamilton, who's the CEO. I suspect
at the margin all of us will be looking to get
slightly different things out of today.
But for me, what I'm really hoping we achieve
is that
I can help you
get to know the Mercury that I know.
The company is an enterprise that
New Zealand can be very, very proud of.
And
it's a facilitator not just of an important service, but
also of economic growth in New Zealand.
We operate intergenerational partnerships with iwi.
We're part of an industry that's investing $10 billion over
the next five years in generation capacity.
And for our part in that,
we're in the middle of a three-project build to the tune of circa
$1 billion of new capacity.
Resilience is obviously a really important part of the equation.
And
in that regard, we've recently announced a circa $700 million
refurbishment of three of our eight dams on
the Waikato system.
We strive to deliver on processes that
support our most vulnerable customers.
We do all of this in a disciplined way on behalf of our
owners, which includes the government,
and many hundreds of thousands of New Zealand KiwiSaver investors.
We will probably in the discussion talk about the trilemma, which
is the necessary link between
sustainability, reliability,
and affordability in a good system.
And we aspire to keep building. We have detailed
plans out to 2030.
We have plans out to 2035, and
we're actively exploring options beyond that.
And we're also working very hard to make
geothermal
a more important proportion of our generation
stack. Stu.
Thanks, Scott. Kia ora koutou. It's great to be back here for a second year.
A year ago, though, when I was here to address the committee, it was on the back of
five record dry months. We had gas and firming
challenges, and that really brought the industry into stark focus.
Gas suppliers challenges certainly remain, but now we have tools being implemented
and considered, such as the strategic energy reserve at the Huntly Power Station,
and we're working to replace gas in the firming mix.
I'm also pleased to report that we're going into winter with lakes at healthy
storage levels. In fact, Lake Taupō is 200% of average
level at this time of the year. And as Scott touched on, Mercury is investing
significantly in renewable energy for New Zealand.
In FY25, over half, in fact, 56% of our
earnings were reinvested into new and existing renewable generation
assets, and we're continuing that momentum through to
FY26. We're on track to deliver our plan of adding three and
a half terawatt hour of new generation by 2030.
That's nearly 10% additional capacity for New Zealand.
That's enough to power an additional 430,000 homes.
We mainly do that through leveraging our strengths in wind and geothermal
development. We're building infrastructure at a pace that's
25% faster than the think big period, with no government
funding, and the scale of our build is demonstrated in one of the pictures that we
sent around that show that we now have just recently built the second
tallest structure in New Zealand.
That's a wind turbine at Kawakawa up in Dargaville.
As Scott mentioned, we also have plans to continue to invest in our hydro system.
We've committed to spending $590 million in our next
three dams, and added to that spend at
Arapuni Dam, it's nearly $700 million over the next decade.
We're also enabling our customers to shift consumption and lower their cost, and
that is a key focus. We're due to launch time of use plans in the next
couple of months, and we're on track to have 50,000 hot water
cylinders under smart management this winter, which is equivalent of about
20 megawatts.
We'll also continue to provide additional support to customers in need, from
tailored support and partnership with community organizations, providing
material support to social retailers to help them continue their important work,
and broadening our support beyond our own customers.
In the industrial space, we continue to enter long-term contracts, which
provide businesses certainty of cost and a guaranteed fuel source for their
operations and also give us confidence to continue to invest in
renewables.
This investment is key to a bright future for New Zealand, and as Scott
noted, given the opportunities ahead of us, we want to continue to work
constructively with industry, with government, to ensure that this future
delivers the best possible outcomes for consumers and for New
ZealandThank you very much. I know you want to get to questions.
Thanks, Scott. Thanks, Jeff. Right. Okay. We'll start. I'll go to Megan.
I'll take say three or four, and then I'll go to someone else.
So, yeah.
Scott, you're next, and then yeah, we'll keep it circulating round.
Yep. I'll stick on the theme for this one.
Look, thank you very much, and thank you for the materials that you've
provided to the committee. They're really comprehensive, so thank you for that.
I just want to start off with the level playing field measures and the work that
the EA is doing in that area. So you've
described the non-discrimination regime
that's being proposed, and yet is not suited to the New
Zealand generation market. What is it about the New Zealand
electricity market that you think means it doesn't deserve
the same protections against those discriminations that apply in other
network sectors in New Zealand?
Yeah, so I believe we are absolutely working through the non-discrimination rules-
... that have been put in place by the EA.
We're working through that now with the board to make sure that we're compliant
with the requirements. I guess it goes further to the
commentary that's around often at the moment in terms of the split of the vertical-
Mm-hmm
... the aggregation that exists currently.
So I think from a, the non-discriminary pricing regime,
we're happy to progress that as has been proposed by the EA and
the Comcom through the energy task force.
It's really how far you take it in terms of vertical desegregation, and that's the
kind of key thing for us that we talk to,
saying that there's significant benefit of having the generation and the retail
components of the business together.
So you're not opposed to an
ASX-traded product as opposed to an
ATC, an OTC rather?
We... Yes, we're supportive of both.
So actually, we are very active in trading,
setting market making when it comes to-
Great
... ASX futures. The EA has just come out recently looking at
putting in place a super peak product.
Again, we've advocated for that, so we're supportive of it, uh, and believe
that-
... that has a key part to play, as does over the counter trading as
well.
Because you have argued in some of your submissions that
mandating an updated OTC
code of conduct instead of the non-discrimination
regime would be preferable. And I was just wondering
if you could say
what are the specific prohibitions in the proposed code
that you'd remove, and what ones would you want put in its place?
Yes. So I have to get... If that goes into the detail-
... I have to come with specific detail for you.
I'm very happy to... Yeah.
Yeah, no, that would be really good.
Because I think we've got two clear kind of different paths there.
One is the bilateral arrangements-
Yep. Yep
... between the gen tailers and the independent retailers.
Yep.
And one is an ASX-
Yep
... market making power. Because I guess one of the things is we have had for
decades now, the independent retailers saying that
the OTC products aren't delivering the fairness
they want to see, which is actually cheaper power bills for consumers.
So I just wondered, if you are advocating for the continuation of a
more bilateral OTC model,
what you think is going to be different in the coming decades than we've seen in
terms of the independent retailers' experience over the last
few decades?
Yeah. Yeah. I think, so certainly if there's a level of detail we need to get into,
we can-
Yeah
... certainly provide that to you.
Yeah.
I think from our perspective, there's good trading that goes on in the
ASX at the moment. We advocated for a super peak product which provides a
level of risk management for different customers that wasn't there in the past.
Mm-hmm.
And that's something we certainly advocated for.
Okay. I'll come back with other ones.
Can we just, if there are other questions, I think Simon, you put your hand up
there. Scott, do you have questions in that area too?
Because I know that's an area of interest to you.
But let's get that bit, that particular bit dealt with and then we'll move on to
other things.
I've got a primary question.
No, that's fine. I think we're going to come-
Can I just ask you then to, just one question because I...
There is a question which we asked, and thank you for your responses to
pre-questions,
which is the, you've got potential positive effects, the splitting
ownership of generation from retail.
Derailing of renewable, the negative effects, you've got derailing of renewable
generation boom.
Can you just walk us through how and why that would be a
problem?
So sorry, just repeat-
Where one of the answers to the splitting
ownership of generation and retail, you said, well, it could derail,
essentially investment in new generation.
Just walk us through why that would be.
Fundamentally it comes down to the best way to
make sure that we're investing at a pace necessary to support New Zealand's growth
is to create certainty in the market.
If you look at the sort of 20 years that existed between
2002 to 2022, there was a significant
amount of uncertainty in the market because demand was pretty flat for 20 years
in effect. We had the risk of TY
being there, not there
from someone sitting in the seat.
So yes, so certainly a TY was at risk as well.
My previous role was to see New Zealand Aluminium Smelter.
And so
That's why I'm laughing.
Yes, that's why So the risk was really there in terms of what's happening in the
market. And if you've got significant risk with big loads that could come
in or go out,
that creates uncertainty to invest because we're spending billions of dollars
in assets that are here for 20, 30, 40 years.
So the best thing for us to do is to have certainty to guarantee that when we spend
it, you know that you're going to get the return over that time.
And so the thing we really talk to there is if there's significant changes
to market settings, to regulatory
settings, really creates uncertainty which can chill
investment. We sort of saw that a little bit when we
talk about something like an Onslow or other things that could be
significantly big impacts on the market, that can have an impact
in terms of chilling. So the main thing for us is to make sure that changes
are well signaled, are evidence-basedAnd are created in
a way which enable us to understand those impacts.
And with that in place, we'll keep building at the pace that we are.
So essentially, what you're arguing there is that if you build
something, you also know that you've got customers because you've got your own
customers as opposed to having to go and find somebody else's.
Yep.
Then you've got a reasonable knowledge that you're going to actually be able to
sell the power.
Correct.
Yeah. And we're yet to see any evidence
that that separation would deliver better outcomes for customers.
But what we can see is the clear link between demand and investment.
Scott, same issue, and then we'll go to-
Same issue
... and then I'll go to Primary over.
Yeah. No, I do have other questions, but just to follow up on that point, I guess,
to the point about certainty, we have had the proposition that the structural
separation
is something that is desirable from a
number of perspectives across Parliament for a couple of years.
So that's been well signaled that that might be
coming.
Does that talk to your point about understanding where the
evolution of the energy sector should go, or do you think that
really what we should be doing is simply
not doing too much and keeping things as they
are
when we have
the energy crisis that we've got at the moment and when we have people who just
can't afford to heat?
Yeah.
I think certainly acknowledge that energy cost is
an issue in New Zealand, and we need to do more in that space.
And so whether that
would come back to electricity costs or fuel costs, there's definitely an
impact on New Zealanders, both businesses and households.
So definitely acknowledge that.
What we're saying is that if we look at the proposal, which is purely
how do we split up the gentailers,
we need to come back to work out what problem are we trying to solve.
And if the problem is trying to come up with
affordable, secure, renewable, or sustainable energy,
I don't believe, and we don't believe, that actually the splitting of the
gentailers is the answer to that problem.
And the reason why that we see that gentailer model being quite
successful, and a number of reports have been conducted that suggest that
the market is working well, it's got some areas to improve in, but it's working
well. The gentailer model basically sets out to say
it really is a way of producing lowest cost,
and smoothing a price out for consumers.
The reason for that is in New Zealand, our
electricity system is largely hydro-dominated.
And it's quite small amounts of storage in hydro.
So that means you can get quite large swings in seasonal
factors on hydro, which can have quite large swings in the
wholesale price of electricity.
That means that if you're a retailer, you actually want to have normally quite
short-term contracts to manage your risk.
That's almost against the generation side of the business, which wants to have
quite long-term contracts to manage, as we just talked
about, the risk of investing for decades.
And so when you have those two mismatches, a good way to manage that is through
vertical integration.
Sup on that. So, my question wasn't really about the merits or not of
structural separation. It's around your point about
creating certainty in the market, and
I think we could
argue the point that
by spending a couple of years fighting the idea of structural
separation or of level playing field measures,
that also creates uncertainty in the market rather than
accepting that we do need to create a market that actually
works. And I think this is where we need the
participation of everyone
to understand that an evolution for electrification is
necessary.
Mm-hmm.
Has to be done. The way we do it, we're always going to come
across with various opinions and
proving the case will be in the doing of the case.
So we do need to accept that there are changes
underway.
If we continue to oppose any change, we
don't make any progress. And I guess that's-
Yeah, just a quick question then, Scott
... the question is if we're going to create certainty,
can't we do it through those discussions formally
rather than simple opposing?
I'd agree. So
certainly we are absolutely up for having that discussion.
I think the issue we have is when people come up with a solution that doesn't
necessarily resolve the problem, and to really understand what problem we're trying
to solve and what are the best things to-
So look, this doesn't answer your question, but through my eyes, the
whole of the Western world's got this infrastructural deficit
issue. We're
here in a city that's perhaps in the eye of that storm.
What I would argue is that the industry structure that we have
is actually delivering for New Zealand in terms of
the infrastructural build that you actually need to see.
Adding uncertainty is not accretive to that.
I think that is maybe the question you were asking.
Okay. I'm going to go to Saps here and...
So when you talk about affordability or affordable and
sustainable, how do you define affordable
electricity for an ordinary household today?
And do you believe your current prices meet the standard?
Good question. And ultimately it comes down to there's two things we look
at. Are we competitive from a global perspective?
But that doesn't necessarily mean it's affordable for New Zealanders.
So when you look at the power prices in New Zealand, we are in the best top
10 from an affordability perspective compared to all the
OECD countries. That's both for household prices and
for small businesses and industrial prices.
So if that's the measure, then we're doing really well.
That doesn't necessarily mean it's affordable for New Zealanders.
If you look at the bill that households get, and you
break that down to the components of it, at the moment, the bill that
a household gets, about $3 per day-Is the
actual electrons that have been produced to go to the household.
So it's $3 a day to generate the electricity going to a household.
That's what it costs at the moment.
By the time you add on transmission, distribution, other costs, it adds up to about
$8 a day.
Now, if you look at over the last few years, prices have been going up higher than
they have previously. A large component of that has been associated with
distribution and transmission costs.
So our job is to make sure that where we are, we have control in
terms of the electrons being produced.
We are producing enough power and building enough power stations
to enable that price to stay pretty flat.
So wait, when you mentioned the breakdown just now, what profit margin are
you targeting, and how much influence do shareholders' return
expectation have on pricing decisions?
So we have
a set of pricing principles that guide what we do in terms
of both our mainly retail prices but also to industrial prices.
That means that we largely pass through lines and distribution costs to our
customers. When you look at the total amount of money that we make,
over half of that goes back into reinvestment in new assets
and existing assets. Another 25% of it
goes to tax and to debt repayment, which means that you're
really left with 25% of what we make being available to our
owners in terms of the returns that they expect on a $10 billion
business.
So is there any specific example or decision you make
when affordability was prioritized over profit?
Sorry.
Any, when you're making a decision whether electricity
affordability-
Yeah
... was prioritized over the profit.
If we build an asset,
if we build a wind farm, we're by and large sourcing that
wind farm in the international market, and it
costs
what it costs. We have to bring that here.
That has to meet our investment hurdles.
If it doesn't meet the investment hurdle, it will not get
built.
Megan.
So a 25% return on an investment would probably
largely, in most sectors, be regarded
as an aggressive strategy in terms of that.
So I guess my question is, we've got lakes at record highs.
Sorry, so it's not 25%-
Well, that was what he said
... it's not 25% return on investment.
Okay. So-
It's of the money that we make in the year-
Yeah
... half of it is reinvested.
Right. Yeah.
25%, and then 25 cents left over as free cash that is-
25% of profit.
Yeah. So, okay. So
I've just got two subs in.
So you're saying that 25% of your profits
are being reinvested into new generation?
No. 50% of our profits are being reinvested back into new generation.
Okay.
Another 25% goes into tax and debt repayment, leaving
25% that's left over for distribution to our owners.
Yeah. So 25% of the income coming in
is being redistributed back to owners.
No. Of net profit.
Yeah, of the profit. Yeah.
Yeah.
No, that's different.
What I'm saying is that in most industries, this would be regarded as an
aggressive strategy. My question is, we've got record
hydro lakes at the moment, where levels in the hydro
lakes.
You've got a plan in terms of your reinvestment in terms of new
generation. Why are consumers not seeing falling power prices?
So if you're a
consumer who is a small medium enterprise that is
on spot price exposure, that is coming down.
If you see the prices at three years out at the moment,
it's gone from about $180 middle of last year, now they're down to
about $130. So definitely the forward curve for power prices is coming down,
and that's representative of a sense of the amount that we're building and
also the risk that's coming down as well. So that's definitely an impact.
What we have in New Zealand, though,
99% of customers are fixed in some form of contracts.
And so that does two... Does the main thing of smoothing those
things out. So whilst the power price in August of 2024
jumped up to $800 per megawatt hour,
99% of customers didn't see that. And so what happens is you have a
smoothed impact, and that smoothed impact happens on the up as well
as on the down. And so you'll see that the forward curve is coming down,
and it'll start to flow through to smoothed contracts.
Yeah. And when will-
And just it's important to get both sides of this.
In that year, in the dry year that Stuart is talking about,
we don't like that.
Yeah.
We get hurt. Our net profit after tax that year
was $1 million.
Mm.
Yep.
So my question is, when will consumers start to see this flow
through in terms of them having reduced power bills, noting
that the EA this week is saying that they are looking
at the charges that the generators, we all
understand the impact that transmission and distribution has in the power bill,
but the EA has specifically said it's going to look at gen
tailers and the way those prices are being passed through.
When can consumers expect to start seeing this?
So yeah, so the work that the EA has announced this week is normal.
This is what you expect a regulator to be doing.
They've done it a couple of years ago, doing it again now.
That's looking at the whole of the gen tailer bill, which does include transmission
distribution as well. So we're certainly participating in that.
But it does come down to, again, the electricity bill that a household
receives has a number of those components in it.
And over the last couple of years, well over half of that increase has been
caused by transmission distribution.
Just noting this week that EA has said they understand what's happening with
distribution and transmission.
Yeah.
They're particularly interested in the generation aspect of it.
Yep. So we'll share that. But basically, the differences that
exist are
modal, so depending on where you are in New Zealand, as well as the makeup of that
bill. So you should expect to see that over the next
few years, the contribution from alliance charges and
distribution chargeDoes start to come down because they are actually locked in
place by the Commerce Commission in terms of what those prices look
like. And then if wholesale prices continue to stay falling, then
that will start to flow through in terms of the ultimate cost that consumers see,
both industrial and household.
So I'll go to Scott in a sec, but so what we would expect to see, you
will start coming... I mean, we're all used to, coming out with what's
our power company telling us the power price is going to go up.
So you're telling us that in the next two, three years, we're going to start seeing
notices coming from our power companies telling us the power price is going to go
down.
Well, I can't predict that power prices will go up or down.
Oh, hang on. Well, that's what you'd like.
All I can say is that we have to manage the investment that needs to keep
taking place in New Zealand as well.
Understand.
So that is a factor we need to take into account.
We're in a boom in terms of spend, and that is something that we have to manage.
But if you were to continue with the model that you talked about,
the half goes to
new generation tax
and
interest payments and so on, and then
a certain amount left for distribution.
If you carry that model, you should still be able to continue doing that and pull
the prices down because you're saying it will cost less.
Not necessarily going to cost less.
I mean, the cost to keep building power plants-
I understand that, yeah
... keeps growing.
Yeah. But you're saying the forward market looks as though it's going to come down.
The forward market is coming down.
So therefore, it should come down.
The wholesale price is coming down.
Yeah. And look, can I just, before I go to Scott, the other thing is, and it's very
hard because your profit in the last two years has changed so
dramatically. Basically, there isn't one in the last year.
But if I took
the 2023/24 year, which didn't
include, of course, the winter of 2024.
So your revenue was $3.4 billion.
Your net profit
after tax was 290, so that's a bit less than 10% of that.
And then you're saying roughly, in a normal year, I know dividends get equalized
over time, that a quarter of that 10%, so about
2.5%, will be going or 2% will be
going in dividends.
Yeah.
So-
What we talk about-
Not 25
... is the difference between EBITDA and profit.
So what we're saying is-
He was doing it higher up.
Yeah. Take the revenue, subtract the cost-
You'll be doing it from EBITDA.
Correct.
Okay.
Yeah.
Right. Well, if that's the case, your EBITDA is...
Oh, that one doesn't
show the EBITDA. Anyway,
I'll go to Scott now.
Thank you, Chair.
No problem.
So you've talked about
the needs. We know under the mixed ownership model that you're
required to generate a profit.
Yeah, and then I pick up the primaries. Yeah.
I'm wondering whether, given the crisis that we're in and
the hardship that
we're seeing experienced both in the manufacturing business sector,
but particularly in households, whether if
the purpose of the mixed ownership model was to provide public
good over a profit, how long would that take for
a cultural change take place, and what type of-
I think there was an effort there
... strategy would you pivot to, do you think, if you
were required to deliver energy as a
public good rather than required to produce a
profit?
He's asking you, does communism work?
And actually, could you also, in that context, tell us what that would do to the
new generation of investors?
One of the things that, apart from choosing our CEO,
one of the most important things that boards do,
is carefully allocate our owners' capital.
Yeah.
Owners
being the Crown, but also KiwiSaver people, institutional investors,
and the like.
And so where those roads lead you to are
things like project analysis and
ensuring that those projects meet hurdles
so that you are not being inefficient with the way that you
allocate
that capital.
And so that's kind of the foundation
stones of the way that we need to operate.
Now, if we were instructed by legislation not
to have those sort of disciplines, that would be
a dramatic and profound change for us.
But not to, it's not to not have those disciplines, but to
focus on delivering affordable,
accessible energy to the community
rather than to focus on generating profit.
Yeah. So I'd say that it's still,
you're really talking about the trilemma again.
And so ultimately, to be able to build power plants,
that's a cost that is set by us buying
international technology, and someone somewhere has to pay for that.
Mm-hmm.
And so basically, you've got to work out what is the way that that is paid for.
At the moment, that's paid through the model we've got, which we pay for it,
invest in it, and then generate a return that enables us to keep investing.
And then what we do in amongst the affordability component is we really look at
our support for customers in a few different ways.
We provide transparency where we can in terms of billing, so people can understand
what the cost is and where it shows up.
We're looking at providing better options for customers so people have the ability
to move loads, change their behaviors, really important.
And then we target a very certain support for
those that are most vulnerable, as customers.
And so our approach really at the moment is those three things, and making
sure that where we have vulnerable customers, that we're supporting them as much as
possible. And so much so that in the last, now it's nearly two
years, we haven't disconnected any customer for a
credit reason. Now, so whilst we're still a commercial business, we
absolutely acknowledge the impact on a certain portion of New Zealand
society and try to support them as much as possible through those credit
situations.
If I could just do a sup on that, Chair.
One.
Okay.
And then I'm going to go to Tangi, and then I'm going to go to Simon for primary.
Primary is great.
Can I go to primary?
I've got primaries all over the place.
I still go to primary, right?
Back over to you.
Yeah. Thank you.
So-
Eventually
... thank you for that. And so you mentioned that you're providing support to
social retailers.
Everybody wants to. Yeah.
The
work that they're doing is really important in the community, and we're also aware
that,
I think you've given some support to C.
Rotman in previous times to look at
hardship in the community.
Some of the gen tailers provide funding for
those who work at the front line to reduce their
exposure to high electricity prices, to insulate their
homes, to go into homes and really solve problems.
Scott, can we get the question quicker, please?
Is that something that you are also looking at,
putting into a collective fund for an energy
wellbeing network, which is-
Okay. All right. Quick next question
... a proposal from Cessa.
I'm not sure if we're doing that work or not, but certainly we have a team of
people that are looking at what can we do to support affordability, especially
for certain portions of New Zealand that find it tough.
I do know banks do work in that space.
Yep.
No, not quite in that space.
But lines. But I'm happy to take this offline.
Yeah, please keep it offline. And Tangi.
Thank you, Mr. Chair. And thank you both.
I appreciate that you've said you're a commercial business, you're 51% owned by
the government.
Mr. Hamilton, what was your remuneration for the last financial year?
For FY25, my total remuneration, so my salary was
$1.15 million, which is base salary.
And then my short-term incentive is
50% of that, and was at target, so about 50% of
1.15 million. That's the fixed
portion of my salary. In addition to that, I get KiwiBank payments,
about 3%,
and long-term incentive wasn't paid out last year.
And all of our compensation details are
in the public domain in excruciating detail in our annual report.
And so, Mr. St. John, what was your remuneration for the last financial year?
Oh, shoot. I couldn't
tell you the exact number, but I think it's

So if we go back a few years ago, our average length of term for a small or
medium business or a industrial customer was about three

So we're seeing that customers are signing up for longer terms to create,
I guess, more certainty,
and actually enable them toGet on with doing the work that they do to keep New

Oh, wow.
Some really great industrial customers, whether they're packaging, whether they're

dairy. You can imagine who they might be.

any benefits or have you seen... What is the

forms of,

three terawatt-hours of energy that's needed to overcome those dry periods.

One is we keep building

who will face big costs to electrify when they've got gas
appliances and gas is no longer available or becomes too expensive?
What's your plans then? Do you think the government needs to be
there to help, say, with a big switch program?
It is an issue for us we're working through.
I don't think we've got the solution at hand now, and I suspect it's going to take
an industry response to be able to do that.
And certainly,
the team, Katherine behind me, and others that are working through how do we come
up with solutions to support that transition away from gas,
whether that's households and businesses.
I don't think we've got a clear answer for that yet, and I think we need to work
through it.
Thank you.
Scott, I think I really appreciate that. It's taken us to time.
We really appreciate the discussion.
There may be some follow-up questions that get sent through-
... which is our normal process.
We've already got some plans.
Sorry?
But we've got some-
And we've got a little bit of information you were going to provide us as well.
Yeah.
But we really appreciate that, and thank you very much.
You're welcome.
Thank you.
Colleagues, we are scheduled to take a break till 1:30,
so you've got the 10 minutes, which was
demanded.
Well done.
Demanded, and we've met it.
Thank you, Scott, for keeping us to time.
Yes. You have one more minute I was going to give you, because I'm going to be
generous.

 Stew Hamilton, Chief Executive
